metadata.title: One Logo, Every Move Remotion Knows.
metadata.description: A technique-showcase reel animating the Musinique logo mark every way Remotion can — 16:9 landscape edition. One mark, 47 SVG paths, 20 techniques. For review: which moves earn their beat?

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. One logo. Forty-seven paths. Twenty techniques. I'm going to run the Musinique mark through every motion move Remotion knows — so you can see which ones actually deserve to live.
Spring Entrance. Default spring physics — mass, stiffness, damping. The mark drops in. Judge the weight: does it feel like something landed, or just appeared?
Overshoot Spring. Low damping, high stiffness. The mark overshoots, rebounds, settles. This is the difference between a physics sim and a cartoon — watch for the squash.
Per-Path Stagger. Forty-seven paths cascade in sequence — each one delayed by a fixed interval. The mark assembles like a score being read bar by bar.
Draw-On Stroke. evolvePath traces each path with a terracotta line, then floods the fill. The mark draws itself — the stroke is the receipt.
Mask Reveal. First a linear wipe sweeps left to right. Then a radial iris opens from center. Two reveals, two reads — which one earns the logo?
Scale Zoom. Linear scale first — uniform, mechanical. Then bezier easing — the mark accelerates and decelerates with intention. Same numbers, different feel.
Rotation. A spring entrance pivot, then continuous slow rotation. Meditative when it works, lazy when it doesn't. This mark has enough geometry to carry it.
Skew And Shear. The mark leans in, holds tension, then releases. Skew is cheap when it's decorative — it earns its place only when it suggests speed or pressure.
Opacity Through Blur. The mark materializes out of a focus fog. Blur and opacity move together — the logo arrives like something coming into range.
Color Interpolation. The mark cycles from warm ink to terracotta and back. This is the one treatment beat — color as the only signal. Does the hue shift say anything, or just shimmer?
Kinetic Scatter. The forty-seven paths explode outward from center, then snap back into register. Forty-seven pieces becoming one — that reunion is the beat.
Glitch Slices. Six horizontal slices offset, then snap clean in eight frames. Aggressive, then gone. Glitch works when it's brief — this is the test.
Trail Echo. The mark slides across frame, leaving ghost copies in its wake. Motion lingers — the trail is the evidence that something moved.
Noise Wobble. Sine-based perturbation in X, Y, and rotation — amplitude fades as the mark settles. Alive when the wobble decays. Anxious when it doesn't.
Elastic Physics. Drop from above, squash at landing, rebound. The mark hits the floor. Spring physics with a mass behind it — this is what weight sounds like when you can only see it.
Path Cascade. The forty-seven paths waterfall in from above — each one a beat behind the last. Top to bottom, the mark rains into frame.
Card Flip. Perspective rotateY — the mark flips like a card being turned over. 3D depth in CSS, no Three.js required. The backface is the terracotta accent.
Shadow Play. An offset duplicate drifts away, then snaps back. The shadow detaches — then grounds. The mark reunites with its own weight.
Composer Summon. The prompt types itself into the Claude composer — then the mark arrives as the result. The interface is always the origin.
Exit Family. Three exits in sequence: shrink-spin, blur-out, mask-close. Exits are underrated — the mark disappearing well is half the edit.
Your turn. Take your own SVG mark. Pick three techniques from this reel. Run them through a Remotion composition at 1920 by 1080, 30 frames per second. Mark each one keep or kill.
Liam, in for Bear.
